EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a quiet first half of the month, storms returned to bring strong wind gusts of 45-60 mph in lower elevations, isolated gusts up to 80 mph in wind prone locations, and around 100 mph for Sierra ridges. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A multi-day storm brought 1.5-3 of rain across the main urban areas of far western NV, with 0.25-1 totals into other portions of northwest NV and east of the Reno-Carson City region to remaining areas west of US-95, with less than 0.25��� near and east of US-95 in west central NV. The highest totals were observed on Christmas Eve. Snowfall in the Sierra from this multi-day event was generally 3-6 feet in areas above 7000 feet, with 1-2 feet down to lake level in the Tahoe Basin. For western NV, snowfall was limited to 1��� or less below 5000 feet, with 1-5��� in foothill locations between 5000-6000 feet, and up to 9��� above 6000 feet near Virginia City.

The backcountry avalanche risk was high per the Sierra Avalanche Center and Warnings were in place.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This winter storm came in a two-stage fashion, first as Pacific moisture is pulled into the Sierra, spreading into western Nevada as rain, and the second piece being a colder shortwave trough. The first portion had snow levels around 5500-6500 feet, with the second piece dropping snow levels to all valley floors.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A colder low pressure moved slowly across the region from the 25th-27th, dropping daytime temperatures to 10-15 degrees below average. Patchy light rain showers developed across much of the region on the 25th, with isolated thunderstorms over west central NV. More widespread rain and snow developed during the late night of the 25th into the 26th, and again the following night, bringing late season snow for the Sierra and a rain-snow mix down to lower elevations of western NV. Snowfall amounts ranged from 2-7��� for the Tahoe basin and into northern Mono County, while in western NV up to 2��� of snow fell (mainly on unpaved surfaces) around the main cities of far western NV, and up to 4��� in Virginia City. An additional 1-5��� of snow fell in the Tahoe basin with up to 8��� in higher elevations during the late night of the 26th into the morning of the 27th (Photos 4 to 6). Rainfall was heaviest across far northeast CA and northwest NV, with several sites receiving between 0.50 and 1 inch, and up to 1.25 inch in parts of the Surprise Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mt Rose Ski Area reported a storm total of 11-15 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Light Sierra snow showers and gusty winds returned on the 19th. A fast moving low pressure system provided a round of light snow showers across the northern Sierra along with gusty southwest winds across western Nevada. The main wave of snow showers moved along the northern Sierra crest early in the afternoon of the 19th with snow levels around 5500-6000'. Snow accumulations were projected to be spotty due to the convective nature of the snow along the incoming cold front. Overall, winds were the main impact with this storm with afternoon wind gusts expected to reach around 30-40 mph for most areas as Sierra ridgetop gusts exceeded 80 mph. This resulted in choppy lake waters and aviation turbulence. Strong crosswinds for high profile vehicles were expected along Hwy 395/I-580 with these gusty winds pushing out into the Basin and Range during the afternoon, impacting area highways including Hwy 95 near Walker Lake.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mesonet station (GLCNV) located 2.3 mile NE of Washoe City, NV, and mesonet station (NV013) Northwest Reno located 2.0 mi NW of Reno, NV, reported peak wind gusts of 66 mph, and 61 mph respectively.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moved across the Pacific Northwest the afternoon through the night of the 24th, and was mainly a wind maker with a few brief showers developing near the Sierra during the day. The strongest gusts reached the Surprise Valley and northern Washoe County where Wind Advisories were hoisted for those areas. This included some strong late night of the 24th with SW-W gusts pushing across the Warner Mountains. Other areas north of US-50 were projected to see wind gusts mainly in the 40-50 mph range with brief 50-60 mph gusts possible in wind prone sites during the afternoon-early evening, with patchy blowing dust downwind of deserts and sinks in northwest-west were less significant with gusts general 35 mph or less.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tractor trailer overturned on southbound I-580 near Bowers Mansion about 2.0 mi SSW of Washoe City, NV. Wind may have been a factor with gusts around 65 mph reported at nearby NDOT wind sensors.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The latest in this series of winter storms impacted the Sierra Feb 3rd through the 5th. This system had lower snow levels, continued strong winds, and flooding concerns across northwest Lassen County. Forecast guidance showed a strong winter storm lasting to the night of 4th until the morning of the 5th. Threats with this storm include strong winds to 60 mph on some valley floors of western NV with blowing dust possible in some basin and Range locations and choppy lake conditions. Heavy mountain snow brought impacts over mountain passes, and valley rain allowed for some possible flooding in portions of Plumas and Sierra counties in addition to the minor flood impacts along the Susan River at Susanville, California.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station (UP983-Mill City) located 1.0 mi W of Mill City, NV, reported a peak wind gust of 87 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An AR supported storm system brought higher elevation snowfall, moderate to heavy valley rain, and strong southwesterly winds to the region the weekend of the 1st. A low pressure system dropping into the Pacific Northwest, supported by a strong coupled jet stream system sporting a 150+ kt jet core, tapped into a plume of subtropical moisture. The resultant moderate to strong AR ensured snow levels remained high around 8000 feet on the 1st through the 2nd of February with periods of moderate to heavy rain falling across lower Sierra elevations and adjacent western NV valleys. The periods of heavier rainfall were also accompanied with stronger winds over the region, especially on the 2nd of February.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public located at the Mount Rose Ski Base NV, and the public located at the Diamond Peak Ski Base, NV, reported 24 hour storm total snowfall amounts of 6.0 and 5.0 inches respectively.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A more significant system moved through the region for the 6th through 8th|with heavy snowfall, strong winds, and falling snow levels. Snow accumulations of 6 to 10 inches below 7000 feet, with 2 to 3 feet possible at the highest mountain peaks were projected for this storm. Dangerous travel conditions were expected over the Sierra passes with this winter storm system. Strong winds also caused hazardous driving conditions for high-profile vehicles, turbulence and low-level wind shear for aviation interests, blown around unsecured objects, potentially blown down tree limbs, and power outages especially overnight on the 6th. Therefore, a Wind Advisory was issued for the Greater Reno- Carson City- Minden Area and Southern Lyon and Mineral counties. A Lake Wind Advisory was also issued for Pyramid Lake with the strong winds in the Sierra overlapping with the Winter Storm Warnings in effect.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mesonet station (DXCNV Sr-431 Diamond Peak), located 2.0 mi NNW of Diamond Peak Ski Base, NV, reported a peak wind gust of 71 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another significant winter storm brought periods of heavy Sierra snow accumulations, rain and light snow to valley areas, and gusty winds overnight on the 13th through the 14th. There was the potential for greater snowfall on Sierra Front valley floors on the 13th with higher snow impacts. Snow showers began to increase in coverage and intensity across the Sierra during the evening of 12th with peak snowfall rates of 2-3/hour forecast for the mid-morning to early afternoon hours on the 13th ahead of an advancing cold front. A few flashes of lightning were also projected on the 13th with best chances (10-15%) in the afternoon and evening. Snow showers lingered through much of the 14th before projected to taper off into the morning of the 15th. Forecast storm total snowfall amounts called for multiple feet along the Sierra crest, around a foot of snow for Tahoe Basin communities (e.g., Truckee, South Lake Tahoe) with up to 2 feet along western Tahoe shores, and 6-12 for foothill areas to include Virginia City by the morning of the 15th. A boom/bust potential increased for valley floors from mid-morning onward on the 13th. The most current hi-res guidance sided with the bust scenario with light snowfall the morning of the 13th with rain by the afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mesonet station (PVPN2 Mogul), located 5.0 miles SW of Stead, NV,  reported a peak wind gust of 69 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure directed an atmospheric river into the Mojave Desert and southern Great Basin, resulting in heavy snow in the mountains. After the atmospheric river had departed, the low passed overhead, and the cold temperatures aloft helped trigger thunderstorms, two of which produced severe winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Rainbow Canyon SNOTEL estimated 21 inches of new snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A colder low pressure system moved across the region on the 25th through the 26th of January, and brought below average temperatures, brisk winds, and increased snow chances through the weekend. An upper trough that dropped south across the Pacific Northwest with cyclogenesis in the base of the trough forming a mid to upper level closed low circulation. This low pressure system then tracked over northern CA, and continued to track south into central CA through the 26th. High resolution (HREF) ensemble simulations projected the formation of bands of light to moderate snow showers starting the morning of the 25th continued overnight into the 26th. Higher, more favorable terrain areas around the eastern Lake Tahoe Basin, the Virginia Range that included Virginia City, and the eastern Sierra into Mono-Southern Lyon-Mineral counties had at least a 30-40% change for five to eight inches of storm total snowfall.|Measurable snowfall chances of 50-60% for something up to an inch or two extended from the Sierra Front east into west-central Nevada.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public located at the Mount Rose Ski Base NV, and the public located at the Diamond Peak Ski Base, NV, reported 24 hour storm total snowfall amounts of 13.0 and 6.0 inches respectively.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong downslope winds were observed southeast of the Ruby mountains during the evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Station (RLKN2) Ruby Lake, Elevation 5970 ft.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quick-hitting winter storm brings gusty winds, mountain snow, and valley rain through this evening. Winter travel impacts were expected for the Sierra with roughly a 3-6 hour period of strong winds expected with this fast-moving storm system. Wind advisories were hoisted for portions of western Nevada and the Eastern Sierra with impacts expected for outdoor recreation, aviation, and travel for high-profile vehicles. A short burst of heavy snow accompanied the frontal passage across the northern Sierra and into the Tahoe Basin between mid-morning and mid-afternoon of the 3rd of January, with snowfall rates expected to exceed 2 per hour at times. A Winter Weather Advisory was issued for portions of northeast California and the Tahoe Basin late morning into this evening. Travel disruptions were anticipated across those areas, especially Sierra passes into the night. Although overall precipitation totals came up across the entire Sierra, the biggest change being a better signal for precipitation expanding further south into Mono County. With the more southerly orientation of the moisture plume with this system, precipitation spillover was significantly reduced into Eastern Sierra communities. Although some locations were anticipated to see advisory-level conditions, it was decided to not expand the Winter Weather Advisory into Mono County due to the main impacts remaining confined to the highest elevations with strong winds being the bigger concern from this storm system.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station (WLKNV-NV DOT) located 6.0 NW of Hawthorne, NV, and mesonet station (EW0588 Yerington) located 5.0 mi ENE of Yerington, NV, reported peak wind gusts of 87 mph, and 66 mph respectively.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The next system on the 6th and 7th Tuesday featured light shower chances across portions of the eastern Sierra and increased east to northeast winds. The next system arrived late Monday as a backdoor cold front that slid south through the area. East to northeast winds behind the front caused upslope flow east of the Sierra crests into the 7th with a 20-30% chance for some light snow showers in the Tahoe Basin area southward into Mono and Mineral counties. The northeast flow from this system was the primary impact with wind gusts upwards of 80+ mph across Sierra ridgetops that brought impacts to backcountry recreation and aviation interests across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mesonet station (SLIDE) located 1.0 mile ESE of Mount Rose Summit, reported a peak wind gust of 58 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent late season system moved through the region from the 11th to 12th. This produced widespread strong, damaging winds throughout western Nevada and the eastern Sierra. This included extensive damage to trees, powerlines, several semi-trailers blowing over, and disruptions to air travel due to wind shear and mountain waves. This storm additionally produced a period of accumulating snowfall along Interstate 80 over Donner Pass, leading to a temporary closure during the afternoon of the 12th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nevada Department of Transportation mesonet 2.3 NE Washoe City and NV Energy mesonet 2 S Minden reported peak wind gusts of 87 kts and 70 kts, respectively.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: April began with a ���fool���s spring��� as Sierra snow shower bands continued with the storm that began in the final days of March. Additional snow amounts ranged from 5-10 inches, with a few higher elevation sites receiving just over a foot of new snow. Elsewhere, high temperatures were only in the 40s for lower elevations while some Tahoe communities remained below freezing on the 1st, about 15-20 degrees below average.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mt Rose Ski area reported 21 to 24 inches of snow for the April portion of the storm with a 3-day storm total of 29 to 34 inches. Diamond Peak Ski area reported 20 inches in the same time frame with a 3-day total of 23 inches. Trained weather spotter in Incline Village reported a storm total of 15 inches for the April portion of the storm, with a 3-day total of 24 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold low pressure aloft over the Desert Southwest helped generate enough instability to fuel isolated thunderstorms, one of which produced severe winds.
An atmospheric river event brought strong/damaging downslope winds to the region along with locally heavy rainfall and high elevation snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts up to 97 mph (NV Energy Site NW Reno), 76 mph (NWS Reno office), 75 mph (Reno Stead Airport), 70 mph (Galena RAWS), 67 mph (Washoe Lake), 63 mph (Little Valley RAWS), and 61 mph (2.7 mi N Minden, NDOT) were recorded. There was also extensive tree, fence, and powerline damage throughout the region. NV Energy reported over 100 unique outages in northwest Nevada and also pre-emptively de-energized 3745 customers for a public safety outage. In total, over 60,000 customers were without power at some point on this day.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level wave moving through N CA/NV provided plentiful forcing for storms to develop on July 30. In fact, showers lingered overnight the night before across portions of the Sierra and NE CA. These storms produced robust outflows with multiple areas of blowing dust and one true haboob/dust storm. We also had flooding that closed a roadway that afternoon/evening along US-395 near the Red Rock area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blowing dust was observed, resulting from thunderstorm outflows, from both the NDOT Camera along US-95 at Luning and the Pilot Peak Alert Camera which points to US-95 just south of Mina. Reports from a spotter in Hawthorne confirmed the dust, but also mentioned visibility around 1-3 miles in that area, not meeting Dust Storm Warning Criteria.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported mud and flooding on SR-439 south of Interstate 80.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding over portions of I-80 near USA  Parkway reported by a member of the public  via social media. The nearest mesonet station recorded 1.41 inches of rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure over Nevada pushed eastward as a low pressure trough moved over Nevada. The trough helped increase strong wind gusts. The trough introduced mid-level level moisture to produce isolated dry thunderstorms. The low pressure system stalled over Nevada the next few days as the high pressure system strengthened and maintained position over central U.S.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Station (D6485) DW6485 Elko, Elevation 5230 ft.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mt Rose Ski resort reported 10 inches of new snow during the 14th. This report was from the Mt Rose Facebook page.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the weekend of the 25th-26th, a strong Pacific storm system brought periods of rain and strong winds across the region. Most of the rain fell over northwest NV and the far western NV foothills. This was day one in a multi-day event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station Slide 0.9 E of Mount Rose Summit recorded a wind gusts of 84 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm system brought high winds to parts of northern Clark County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Gusts exceeding 60 mph occurred across portions of the Nevada National Security Site, with a peak of 64 mph at Desert Rock Airport (KDRA).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a quiet first half of the month, storms returned to bring strong wind gusts of 45-60 mph in lower elevations, isolated gusts up to 80 mph in wind prone locations, and around 100 mph for Sierra ridges. Rainfall was limited into northwest Nevada, with the highest totals near the Oregon border. Snow levels were quite high, above 9000 feet for this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station at Gerlach recorded a wind gust of 57 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A multi-day storm brought 1.5-3 of rain across the main urban areas of far western NV, with 0.25-1 totals into other portions of northwest NV and east of the Reno-Carson City region to remaining areas west of US-95, with less than 0.25��� near and east of US-95 in west central NV. The highest totals were observed on Christmas Eve. Snowfall in the Sierra from this multi-day event was generally 3-6 feet in areas above 7000 feet, with 1-2 feet down to lake level in the Tahoe Basin. For western NV, snowfall was limited to 1��� or less below 5000 feet, with 1-5��� in foothill locations between 5000-6000 feet, and up to 9��� above 6000 feet near Virginia City.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained weather spotter reported 5 inches of snow in New Washoe City, with 9 inches locally in the highlands of Virginia City, and 1 inch at the NWS Reno office at 4950 feet. We also had spotter and/or CoCoRaHS reports of 4.5 inches 3 WNW of Lakeview and 4 inches 4 W of Reno.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: By the middle of the month, a wet and stormy pattern returned the region. This caused several days of strong winds, with gusts upwards of 80 mph on the Sierra ridges. Additionally, light snow was reported in the Sierra, with overall light amounts above 7,000 feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station Slide 0.9 E Mount Rose (DRI) recorded a wind gust of 72 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: By the middle of the month, a wet and stormy pattern returned the region. This caused several days of strong winds, with gusts upwards of 80 mph on the Sierra ridges. Additionally, light snow was reported in the Sierra, with overall light amounts above 7,000 feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mount Rose Ski Resort reported 1 to 3 inches of snow in the last 24 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rain spread across far western NV from the 13th into the morning of the 14th. The next wave of moisture moved across the region from the evening of the 15th into the 16th, with another round occurring on the 17th into the early morning of the 18th. Snow levels during this event were generally above 7,000 feet, with between 6 to 12 inches of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mount Rose Ski Resort recorded 4 to 6 inches of snow in the 24 hour period ending on the 18th. The 48 hour storm total was 11-18 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The final storm of this series brought colder conditions with a rain-snow mix down to 4500-5000 feet in parts of western NV on the 20th. Rainfall totals from this series of storms were between 0.50��� and 2��� across western NV , with liquid totals between 2-5��� for much of the eastern Sierra. Sierra snow totals were generally 1-2 feet above 7000 feet but amounts below 7000 feet were minimal, generally 2��� or less.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mount Rose Ski Resort recorded 6 inches of snow at the ski base and 8 inches at the top.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system of tropical origin moved northward into the area and combined with deep moisture to produce an afternoon of thunderstorms. These storms produced accumulating hail and heavy rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak low pressure area approaching from the west tapped into weak mid-level instability, fueling light showers. These showers evaporated into dry subcloud air with steep lapse rates, triggering severe downdrafts.
